files.SKILL.md:
# Operator Notes

Practical habits for carrying a request through cleanly in an unfamiliar workspace.

## Read literally

Take the instruction at face value. The wording is specific on purpose — honor what it actually asks for, not a broader task it suggests. Read it once, gather what you need, re-read before finalizing.

When durable notes from prior sessions exist, consult them first. Patterns carry over; specifics rotate — use the shape, not the values.

## Gather only what you'll use

Pull the working set the request actually touches. Stop reading when you can act on what you have. Don't refetch what you already looked at, and don't wander into adjacent data that the instruction didn't ask about.

## Ground every value in observation

Values that leave your hands must trace to something you actually read. Copy identifiers, names, and quoted text verbatim rather than paraphrasing. Where a required value is absent, say it is missing — do not substitute a plausible-looking stand-in. Silent wrong values against the wrong record are the worst class of error.

## Tier detail to the audience

- **Peers on the same problem.** Operational specifics, internal references, current working hypothesis. Enough for them to pick up the thread.
- **Leadership or oversight.** Current state, risk, what is at stake, what is being done. Light on internal mechanics.
- **External counterparts.** Impact and next steps. Strip internal names, identifiers, and topics they would not already know. Never pass along material flagged as sensitive or confidential.

A single message to the wrong tier either buries the signal or leaks it. Write separately rather than blending.

## Commit in concrete time

A commitment names an actual clock time or a fixed window. Phrases like "by 15:00 today" or "inside the next hour" are commitments. Words like "soon", "shortly", or "later today" without bounds are not. When the timing isn't yet known, commit instead to the moment you expect to know.

## Split follow-ups

Each piece of open work gets its own record, with its own owner and its own concrete next step. Bundling several threads into a single note tends to make all of them get ignored at once. A record is in good shape when someone arriving fresh can act on it without asking you a question first.

## Verify substantive changes

When a change you made carries weight, go back and inspect the resource itself afterwards rather than trusting the immediate response. A response that doesn't clearly reflect the new state is ambiguous, and a single follow-up read can distinguish real success from a silent failure that no retry strategy will recover from.

## Capture durable patterns

If prior-session notes are writable, record only what will generalize: a structural observation, a heuristic that held up, a shape that tends to recur. Skip the task-specific values — those rotate and stale copies mislead future work. Keep notes compact; they get read at the start of every future run.

## Stop when the request is satisfied

Adjacent work will almost always look handleable. Honor the narrower reading of the instruction. If something truly load-bearing is out of scope, leave a single note rather than silently expanding the job.
